name: EROS — Extremely Reliable Operating System
summary: Jonathan Shapiro et al. (Univ. of Pennsylvania, late 1990s): academic predecessor to seL4 in the L4/KeyKOS capability-microkernel lineage. Capability-only addressing of all kernel objects; persistent capability spaces with checkpoint/restart; the 'red line' between trusted and untrusted code is drawn entirely by capability holding. Discontinued; seL4 is the verified descendant that reached production.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
**EROS** (Extremely Reliable Operating System; Jonathan Shapiro et al., University of Pennsylvania, late 1990s) is the academic predecessor in the lineage that produced seL4. EROS implements pure capability-based addressing: every kernel object — segments, processes, capabilities themselves — is named only by a capability, and there is no ambient namespace.

EROS descends from **KeyKOS** (KeyLogic / Tymshare / Key Logic, 1980s+) — a commercial capability OS used in banking and timesharing — and adapts the same discipline to a research microkernel design suitable for academic study and verification. The KeyKOS / EROS / Coyotos / seL4 chain is the through-line of capability-OS research from the 1980s to the present.

sections.title: Persistent capabilities and the red line
sections.content:
EROS's distinctive contributions:

- **Single-level store with persistent capabilities**: the entire system state — including processes and their capability holdings — is checkpointed transparently to disk. Restart restores capabilities exactly as they were, so capability-based authority survives reboots; no separate filesystem-with-permissions layer is needed because *capabilities are the persistent objects*.
- **The 'red line'** between trusted and untrusted code is drawn entirely by capability holding. EROS made this argument concretely: in a capability OS, the TCB (trusted computing base) is exactly the code that holds high-authority capabilities; everything else is provably non-privileged because it lacks the capabilities to do anything privileged.
- **Constructor pattern**: a constructor is a capability that, when invoked, instantiates a new protected service with a known set of held capabilities. The constructor is itself non-confining (it knows what it builds) but *what it builds* can be confined — the standard ocap pattern of "the factory is auditable, the products are bounded."

sections.title: Status and the path to seL4
sections.content:
Status (as of 2026-04): EROS itself is **discontinued/historical**. Coyotos (Shapiro et al., 2006) was the intended successor, attempting verification with BitC; it did not reach production. The verified descendant that did reach production is **seL4** (Klein, Heiser et al., NICTA / Data61), which inherits the EROS / L4 design discipline and adds a complete formal proof of authority confinement.

The lesson EROS established for later capability OSes: **capability-only kernels are practical and the security argument is clean**, but unverified C-level implementations leave too much trust on the table. seL4's contribution was to take EROS-class design and pay the verification cost. Conversely, seL4's design only became economically attractive *because* EROS had proven the capability-only kernel design space was a winnable target.

Reference influence: KeyKOS / EROS lineage is acknowledged in Joe Duffy's Midori writeups, in seL4's design papers, and in the Spritely / Agoric ocap community as the operating-system pillar of the same intellectual tradition that gave us E and Newspeak at the language level.
